title: Trimming a foot ring on the wheel
subtitle: Cutting a recessed ring into the base of a leather-hard thrown piece
excerpt: Trimming removes excess clay from the base of a thrown piece at the leather-hard stage and cuts a recessed foot ring that lifts the pot off the shelf and refines its base profile. Done on the wheel with a loop tool, with the piece mounted upside-down.
sourceNotes: Wheel trimming technique derived from first principles.

content.text: After throwing, the base of a pot carries excess clay: the floor is thick from the opening stage, and the outside base is bulged outward from the pull.
. The piece is mounted upside-down on the wheel head. Trimming too soon tears the clay; trimming too late requires scraping rather than cutting.

The piece is ready to trim when it holds its shape, rings slightly when tapped, and feels cool-to-cold on the cheek. Soft, warm clay is too wet; hard, cold, pale clay is past the trimming window. For 500 g of stoneware in average room temperature, trimming is typically possible 8-24 hours after throwing.

Place the piece upside-down on the wheel head with the rim resting on the clay surface. Centre it by spinning the wheel slowly and tapping the piece gently toward centre until it runs true; no wobble visible from the side. Secure it in place by pressing three small coils of soft clay around the rim at equal intervals, or use a chuck.

Spin the wheel slowly. Hold the needle tool against the spinning base to mark a circle at the inner edge of the foot ring. The foot ring should sit inside the outer wall. Say the pot is 80 mm across at the base. The foot ring outer edge might be at 70 mm and the inner edge at 55 mm. That gives a ring about 7 to 8 mm wide.

With the wheel at medium speed, hold the loop tool against the outer edge of the base and remove the clay that lies outside the planned foot ring. Work in light cuts, taking 2-3 mm at a time. The outside of the pot's base should curve smoothly inward toward the foot ring, not a flat pad.
Hold the loop tool at the inner marked line and cut a groove straight down to the depth of the foot ring, typically 4-6 mm. This groove defines the inner wall of the foot ring.

Remove the clay inside the inner groove across the base of the pot, working outward toward the groove. This creates the recessed area inside the foot ring and reduces the base weight. Stop before breaking through the pot floor; leave at least 6 mm.
What it looks like when it is right

The foot ring is a clean raised band, level, with a flat standing surface and crisp cut edges on both sides. When the pot is turned right-side up, the base profile curves smoothly without a thick pad. The wall thickness should feel consistent from the outside of the foot ring to the pot wall.

items.fix: Trim at true leather-hard; hold the loop tool at a low, shallow angle to shave rather than dig
items.cause: Clay is too dry for cutting, or tool held at too steep an angle
items.symptom: Loop tool chatters and tears the surface

items.fix: Check base thickness by tapping; a dull thud means adequate clay, a hollow ring means too thin, if through-trimmed the piece is unusable
items.symptom: Base becomes too thin and the pot knocks hollow

items.fix: True the piece before trimming; a rib held flat against the spinning foot ring can identify and correct high spots
items.cause: Piece was not properly centred upside-down before trimming
items.symptom: Foot ring is not level; pot rocks on a flat surface
